open_questions: Is the orchestrator actually a V8 isolate? No source confirms this term. Official docs say 'JavaScript script' in an 'isolated environment'; the community runtime reference says 'VM sync timeout' and 'no Node.js APIs in the orchestrator', which points to a vm-module / VM-context sandbox rather than a separately provisioned V8 isolate. The exact engine (Node's vm module, a QuickJS-style embed, or a true isolated-vm V8 isolate) is unverified.

The concurrency formula min(16, max(2, cores−2)) comes only from one community-reverse source (the workflow-creator skill). Official docs say only 'up to 16, fewer on limited cores.' The exact arithmetic, the floor of 2, and whether it counts physical vs logical cores are unconfirmed against Anthropic.

Is there an AST/static-analysis gate (no imports / no dunders / banned names) enforced before execution, or are the banned calls (Math.random, Date.now, require, fs, process, network) caught only at runtime by throwing? The community reference describes them as throwing; whether validation is pre-parse static AST gating or runtime trap is not definitively stated for the official product.

Does the worktree-isolation cost (~200–500 ms + disk per agent) scale with repo size, and what exactly is copied in (only .worktreeinclude gitignored files, plus the git checkout)? The 200–500 ms figure is a single community estimate, not benchmarked across repo sizes.

Are completion notifications ever surfaced as OS-level / push notifications for long (hours/days) background workflows, or only as in-conversation <task-notification> injection? Sources confirm in-conversation injection (issue #18544) but the OS/desktop notification path for workflows specifically is not documented.

How do the documented WorkflowAgentCapError / WorkflowBudgetExceededError error types and the wf_… run-ID scheme appear in the official product? These names are from a community-reverse reference and could differ from shipped internals.

The token 'budget' object (budget.total/spent()/remaining()) and the workflow() one-level-nesting rule are community-reverse only — whether they are exactly the shipped public surface, or an authoring-skill abstraction, is unconfirmed.

Whether subagents spawned by a workflow can themselves spawn further subagents — official sub-agents docs state 'Subagents cannot spawn other subagents,' but it is unverified whether workflow-spawned agents are a special case.

findings.topic: Claude Code Dynamic Workflows — community analyses, usage patterns, orchestration patterns, framework comparisons, and documented limitations (launched ~2026-05-28, research preview)
findings.summary: Claude Code Dynamic Workflows (research preview, shipped 2026-05-28 alongside Claude Opus 4.8, requires Claude Code v2.1.154+) invert control flow: instead of the LLM deciding the next step turn-by-turn, Claude WRITES a JavaScript orchestration script that a separate runtime executes in the background. The script owns loops/branching/fan-out and holds intermediate results in script variables; only the final verified answer reaches the conversation context. Leaf agent() calls spend tokens and each runs in an isolated, discarded context window. Official primitives/limits are confirmed by docs (16 concurrent agents, 1000 total per run, resumable within a session, /deep-research bundled, /workflows monitor, /effort ultracode, save to .claude/workflows/). The deepest API-level details (exact signatures for agent/parallel/pipeline/phase/log/budget/workflow, schema-forced structured output, determinism guard that THROWS on Math.random()/Date.now(), the agent()-call journal enabling resume) come from a community-authored skill repo (ray-amjad/claude-code-workflow-creator) and the alexop.dev deep-dive — these are community-inferred / reverse-engineered, not in the public docs, though they align with official behavior statements. IMPORTANT TRIANGULATION NOTE for this project: the official runtime is JavaScript; the named pattern catalog (fan-out, pipeline-by-default, loop-until-budget, loop-until-dry, adversarial verify, judge panel, diverse-lens verify, nested workflow) is canonical from the community skill repo. "Completeness critic" and "multi-modal sweep" are looser community framings (MindStudio), not in the canonical catalog. Real usage is real but early: Jarred Sumner (Bun) used it for a Zig-to-Rust port (do-work then adversarial-review-then-apply, two reviewers per file, hundreds of parallel agents), and Klarna found dead code static analysis missed. The dominant documented critique on Hacker News is token cost ("tokenmaxxing" — users hit Max caps fast: 90 agents, or 62 Opus agents burning a 5-hour cap in 18 min), plus "slop debt", weak human-in-the-loop control mid-run, and skepticism that mechanical refactors prove the feature. Versus LangGraph/CrewAI/AutoGen: workflows are dev-time, model-authored, script-as-orchestrator with runtime-decided fan-out; LangGraph is a production, human-authored, explicitly-typed DAG with persistent cross-session state — so workflows trade auditability/strict determinism for emergent decomposition.

mechanisms.name: Control-flow inversion: script-as-orchestrator (not LLM turn-by-turn)
mechanisms.how_it_works: Claude writes a JavaScript orchestration script from the natural-language request; a separate runtime executes it in the background while the session stays responsive. The script owns the loop, branching, and fan-out; intermediate results live in script variables instead of Claude's context window. Only the final verified answer lands in the conversation. Official docs table: 'Who decides what runs next' = 'The script' (vs Claude turn-by-turn for subagents/skills); 'Where intermediate results live' = 'Script variables'.

mechanisms.name: Leaf agent() calls in isolated, discarded contexts
mechanisms.how_it_works: Only leaf agent() calls spend model tokens; each subagent runs in its own clean context window, receives a specific task, returns a result to the script, and its context is discarded. Subagents do not communicate or share state with each other (unlike Agent Teams). They always run in acceptEdits mode and inherit the session tool allowlist regardless of session permission mode.

mechanisms.name: Concurrency and agent caps (16 concurrent / 1000 total)
mechanisms.how_it_works: Up to 16 concurrent agents (fewer on low-core machines — community skill states min(16, max(2, cores-2))), to bound local resource use. Hard cap of 1000 agent() calls per run to prevent runaway loops (community skill: throws WorkflowAgentCapError). Excess concurrent calls queue until slots free.

mechanisms.name: Resume via per-agent result journal (content-cached)
mechanisms.how_it_works: The runtime tracks/journals each agent's result as the run progresses; on stop-and-resume, already-completed agents return cached results and the rest run live. Resume works only within the same Claude Code session — exiting Claude Code mid-run restarts the workflow fresh next session. Community analysis frames this as the workflow journaling every agent() call so a run can resume.

mechanisms.name: Determinism guard: non-deterministic calls THROW
mechanisms.how_it_works: Because the run is journaled for resume, non-determinism would invalidate the cache, so the runtime makes Math.random(), Date.now(), and argless new Date() throw inside a workflow. Host access (require, fs, process, network) is also blocked. Workarounds: pass timestamps via args; vary prompts/labels by loop index; new Date(specificValue) is allowed. This is the community-reverse 'fail-loud determinism guard' framing; official docs confirm the no-filesystem/no-shell-from-script constraint but the specific throwing-functions list is from the community skill and alexop.dev.

mechanisms.name: Orchestration primitives: agent / parallel / pipeline / phase / log / workflow / budget / args
mechanisms.how_it_works: Community-documented signatures (skill repo + alexop.dev): agent(prompt, opts?) with opts {schema (JSON Schema forcing validated structured output, retries on mismatch), label, phase, model ('haiku'|'sonnet'|'opus'|'inherit'|id), isolation:'worktree', agentType, stallMs}. parallel(thunks) = barrier; expects [() => agent(...)], throwing thunks resolve to null (never rejects). pipeline(items, ...stages) = NO barrier; items stream stage-to-stage, each stage gets (prevResult, originalItem, index); throwing stage -> that item becomes null. workflow(nameOrRef, args?) = inline subworkflow, ONE level deep only. phase(title)/log(msg) = progress UI. budget.total / budget.spent() / budget.remaining() for token-target introspection. args = serialized tool input. These signatures are NOT in the public docs.

mechanisms.name: Pipeline-by-default (parallel only as an explicit barrier)
mechanisms.how_it_works: Community guidance: default to pipeline() because items advance the moment each is ready (no head-of-line blocking). Use parallel() barriers ONLY when the next stage needs the entire prior result set — to dedup/merge, early-exit on a total, or reference 'the other findings'. Anti-pattern flagged: parallel -> transform-with-no-cross-item-dependency -> parallel should have been a pipeline. Not flattening/filtering (do that inside a pipeline stage).

mechanisms.name: Adversarial verification pattern
mechanisms.how_it_works: For each finding, spawn N independent skeptic agents prompted to REFUTE it; keep the finding only if a majority survive (e.g. 3 votes/claim, survives if fewer than 2 refutations). Officially described in docs/blog as 'independent agents adversarially review each other's findings' and 'other agents try to refute what they found, and the run keeps iterating until the answers converge.' The N-skeptic / majority-vote implementation detail is community-documented. Diverse-lens variant: give each verifier a distinct lens (correctness/security/performance/reproducibility) instead of N identical ones.

mechanisms.name: Judge panel pattern
mechanisms.how_it_works: When the solution space is wide and single-attempt-iterated is weak: generate N independent attempts from different angles, score them with parallel judge agents, then synthesize from the winner while optionally grafting the best parts of runners-up. Officially gestured at by 'draft a plan from several angles and weigh them against each other.' Named/structured form is from the community pattern catalog.

mechanisms.name: Loop-until-budget / loop-until-dry / loop-until-target patterns
mechanisms.how_it_works: Loop-until-target: counter loop, e.g. while(bugs.length < 10). Loop-until-budget: while(budget.total && budget.remaining() > 50_000) to scale depth to the user's token target. Loop-until-dry: keep spawning finders until K consecutive rounds surface nothing new (dryRounds counter + round cap); critical gotcha — dedupe against everything SEEN, not just confirmed results, or the loop never terminates. All loops must carry a hard stop (counter/budget). Example in skill: dead-code-sweep.js.

mechanisms.name: Completeness-critic loop
mechanisms.how_it_works: A critic agent reads artifacts and emits harsh findings; a fixer implements exactly what the critic found; they loop until the critic says APPROVED or a hard cap (e.g. 5 rounds) is hit. Catches errors single-pass review misses. This is a community framing (MindStudio / general agentic-pattern writeups) layered on top of the adversarial-verify idea; it is NOT a named primitive in the canonical pattern catalog and the '5 rounds max' is illustrative, not an official constant. Skill repo's implement-and-review.js shows the do/while + MAX_ROUNDS shape.

mechanisms.name: Multi-modal / comprehensive-sweep fan-out
mechanisms.how_it_works: Decompose a comprehensive job so every part is covered in parallel (e.g. 'review every file in this diff', 'audit all 40 dependencies', 'audit every endpoint under src/routes/ for missing auth'). Officially these are the headline use cases (codebase-wide bug sweep, 500-file migration, security/auth audits). 'Multi-modal sweep' as a discrete named pattern is a loose community label, not in the canonical catalog — the underlying mechanic is fan-out-then-synthesize / pipeline.

mechanisms.name: Schema-forced structured output as the reliable data path
mechanisms.how_it_works: Passing opts.schema (a JSON Schema) forces the subagent to call a structured-output tool; validation happens at the tool-call layer so the model retries on mismatch. Community guidance: every result field you later read off should be backed by a schema const. Schema/model/isolation/agentType changes invalidate the journal cache (force re-run); label/phase are cosmetic.

mechanisms.name: Triggering, monitoring, saving, and ultracode
mechanisms.how_it_works: Trigger by including the word 'workflow' in a prompt (alt+w or backspace to dismiss the highlight; can be disabled in /config), running a bundled command (/deep-research), or /effort ultracode (combines xhigh reasoning + automatic workflow orchestration for every substantive task; session-scoped, resets next session). Monitor with /workflows (phases show agent count, token total, elapsed; p=pause, x=stop, r=restart agent, s=save). Save a run's script to .claude/workflows/ (project, shared) or ~/.claude/workflows/ (personal); project wins on name collision. Disable via /config toggle, settings.json disableWorkflows:true, CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_WORKFLOWS=1, or org managed settings.

mechanisms.name: Documented limitations and failure modes
mechanisms.how_it_works: (1) Token cost is the #1 critique — 'tokenmaxxing'; HN users hit Max caps fast (one hit cap first-time on a small package review with 90 agents; another burned a 5-hour cap in 18 min with 62 Opus agents). (2) No mid-run user input except agent permission prompts — for stage sign-off, run each stage as its own workflow; users want nudge/correction injection mid-run. (3) Resume only within the same session. (4) 'Slop debt' / noise accumulation — LLM-cleaning-up-after-LLM; users add deterministic gates (cyclomatic complexity grading). (5) Not for strict determinism (fan-out decided at runtime) or precise audit logs of which agents ran when — hand-orchestrate or use custom multi-agent systems for those. (6) Permission UX: without Auto mode, permission prompts can stall parallelism; subagents force acceptEdits. (7) Enterprise ships OFF by default (admin enable). (8) Naming concern: 'workflow' collides with Temporal/existing orchestration terms and is a domain-standard word, causing false keyword triggers.

mechanisms.name: Real-world usage anecdotes (Bun port, Klarna)
mechanisms.how_it_works: Jarred Sumner (Bun creator) used dynamic workflows + adversarial code review to port Bun from Zig to Rust: one workflow mapped the correct Rust lifetime for every struct field, the next wrote each .rs file as a behavior-identical port; hundreds of agents in parallel with two reviewers per file; he banned slow commands (git, cargo) inside the agent loop for throughput; per-unit pattern = do-work, adversarial-review, apply. ~750K lines Rust, 99.8% of the existing test suite passing — but canary-only / not yet in production. HN skeptics noted mechanical refactors are agent-friendly anyway, one called a generated test the worst test-repeats-implementation they'd seen, and one flagged team difficulty navigating ~1M lines of generated Rust. Klarna reportedly found dead code that traditional static analysis missed.

mechanisms.name: Comparison vs LangGraph / CrewAI / AutoGen
mechanisms.how_it_works: Workflows: model-AUTHORED JS script as orchestrator, dev-time, runtime-decided fan-out, results in script variables, resumable within-session, optimized for emergent decomposition + adversarial verification at 10s-100s of agents. LangGraph: human-authored, explicit typed DAG (TypedDict/Pydantic), production runtime, persistent cross-session state, conditional edges, hard human-in-the-loop checkpoints — chosen when you need strict determinism, auditability, or hot-swappable agents. CrewAI = role/crew metaphor (sequential specialist handoff), reportedly ~3x LangGraph token cost; AutoGen = conversation/critique metaphor. Common community take: not mutually exclusive — use Claude Code/workflows to BUILD the LangGraph/CrewAI app, or use workflows for breadth+verification and a framework for stateful production orchestration.

open_questions: LANGUAGE MISMATCH for this project: official docs and every community source describe the workflow runtime as JavaScript. The langchain-dynamic-workflow project is a Python port to deepagents — the JS-vs-Python boundary, idioms (thunks, Date.now throwing), and any semantic gaps were not found documented anywhere; the Python port's design choices are this repo's own, not Anthropic's.

Exact agent()/parallel()/pipeline()/budget signatures, the constants (min(16,max(2,cores-2)), 512KB script cap, 180s stall x5 retries, 30s VM sync timeout, WorkflowAgentCapError/WorkflowBudgetExceededError), and the throwing-determinism list come ONLY from a community-authored skill (ray-amjad) and alexop.dev — NOT from official docs. They align with official behavior statements but should be treated as reverse-engineered until Anthropic publishes an API reference. The ray-amjad README itself called the Workflow tool 'unreleased and unannounced' at authoring time.

Whether the journal is genuinely content-hash-based (as the langchain port's design assumes) vs index/position-based is not stated in official docs; community sources describe per-agent result caching keyed on schema/model/isolation/agentType changes but do not confirm a content hash of the prompt.

Bun-port timeline is inconsistent across sources: 'six days' (alexop.dev, claudefa.st headline, Digg title) vs 'eleven days from first commit to merge' (MarkTechPost / Sumner's own numbers). Likely six days of active rewrite vs eleven to merge, but not reconciled by a primary Sumner statement we located.

'Completeness critic' and 'multi-modal sweep' as discrete named patterns appear only in looser community writeups (MindStudio), not in the canonical pattern catalog (which uses fan-out, pipeline, loop-until-*, adversarial-verify, judge-panel, diverse-lens-verify, nested-workflow). Whether the engine treats them as first-class patterns or just compositions is unconfirmed.

Convergence criteria for adversarial verification ('iterate until answers converge') are described qualitatively in official docs; the concrete majority-vote / N-skeptic thresholds are community implementation choices, and the actual built-in /deep-research voting threshold (e.g. '3-vote per claim, survives if <2 refutations') is community-reported from inspecting a run, not officially specified.

No independent, quantitative benchmark of dynamic-workflow output QUALITY (vs single-agent or vs LangGraph/CrewAI pipelines) was found — all framework comparisons are qualitative or about token efficiency in general, not head-to-head on workflow results. The 99.8% Bun test-pass figure is self-reported and the build was canary-only/not in production at time of writing.

verified_claims.claim: A dynamic workflow is a JavaScript script (NOT Python) that a runtime executes in the background; the script owns loops/branching/fan-out and intermediate results live in script variables, so only the final answer reaches the conversation context.
verified_claims.evidence: Official docs state verbatim: 'A dynamic workflow is a JavaScript script that orchestrates subagents at scale. Claude writes the script for the task you describe, and a runtime executes it in the background while your session stays responsive.' The comparison table confirms 'Who decides what runs next: The script' and 'Where intermediate results live: Script variables.' Anthropic's Boris Cherny (HN bcherny) further confirmed the runtime is 'JavaScript, running locally or in the cloud' and 'built into the Claude Agent SDK.' IMPLEMENTER NOTE: the official language is JavaScript; this repo's Python/deepagents surface is a community port, not an official Python API.

verified_claims.claim: Concurrency cap is 'up to 16 concurrent agents, fewer on machines with limited CPU cores'; the precise formula min(16, max(2, cores-2)) is the exact arithmetic.
verified_claims.evidence: The 16-ceiling + per-core reduction is OFFICIAL, verbatim: 'Up to 16 concurrent agents, fewer on machines with limited CPU cores' (rationale 'Bounds local resource use'). The specific formula min(16, max(2, cores-2)) is NOT in official docs — it appears only in the ray-amjad api-reference limits table ('Concurrent agents | min(16, max(2, cores - 2))'). So the cap value is confirmed; the exact cores-2 arithmetic and floor-of-2 are single-source community-reverse and should be treated as plausible-but-unconfirmed.

verified_claims.claim: Hard cap of 1,000 agents total per run, to prevent runaway loops (community: throws WorkflowAgentCapError).
verified_claims.evidence: Official docs limits table: '1,000 agents total per run' with rationale 'Prevents runaway loops.' Independently corroborated by multiple third-party outlets (MarkTechPost, Pasquale Pillitteri, Ken Huang). The specific error class name WorkflowAgentCapError is community-only (ray-amjad: 'Lifetime agent() calls per run | 1000 | Throws WorkflowAgentCapError') and unconfirmed by Anthropic — the cap NUMBER is confirmed, the error-class name is not.

verified_claims.claim: The workflow script itself has NO direct filesystem or shell access; only the leaf agents read/write/run commands, and the script merely coordinates them.
verified_claims.evidence: Official docs limits table verbatim: constraint 'No direct filesystem or shell access from the workflow itself', reason 'Agents read, write, and run commands. The script coordinates the agents.' This is the core control-flow-inversion / I-O-split property and is unambiguously official.

verified_claims.claim: Resume is same-session-only: completed agents return cached results and the rest run live, but exiting Claude Code mid-run restarts the workflow fresh next session.
verified_claims.evidence: Official docs verbatim: 'If you stop a run, you can resume it: agents that already completed return their cached results, and the rest run live.' and 'Resume works within the same Claude Code session. If you exit Claude Code while a workflow is running, the next session starts the workflow fresh.' The comparison table also lists workflows as 'Resumable in the same session.' Fully official. (Implication: completed-agent results are session/in-memory scoped, not durably persisted across restarts — the durability detail itself is inferred, but the same-session-only behavior is explicit.)

verified_claims.claim: The runtime tracks each agent's result to enable resume, and the resume cache key is a content hash of each agent() call's (prompt, opts) — with schema/model/isolation/agentType baked into the key (changing any re-runs the call; label/phase are cosmetic).
verified_claims.evidence: OFFICIAL (behavior only): 'The runtime tracks each agent's result as the run progresses, which is what makes a run resumable.' The specific MECHANISM — a content hash of (prompt, opts) as the key — is community-only. ray-amjad states the journal is 'keyed by a hash of its (prompt, opts)' and that 'schema, model, isolation, and agentType are the four options baked into the resume cache key.' Notably, alexop.dev does NOT state the key is a content hash of (prompt, opts) — it only says 'Workflows journal every agent() call so a run can resume.' So the content-hash-of-(prompt,opts) keying rests on a SINGLE reverse-engineering source (ray-amjad); Anthropic never publishes the key derivation. Whether it is content-hash vs index/position-based is officially undocumented.

verified_claims.claim: A fail-loud determinism guard makes Date.now(), Math.random(), and an argument-less new Date()/Date() THROW inside a workflow (new Date(specificValue) still works); host access (require/fs/process/network) is also blocked.
verified_claims.evidence: This is the single highest-stakes community claim and is NOT in official docs. It is attested by two independent community sources. alexop.dev (hands-on observation, not citing Anthropic): 'Date.now(), Math.random(), and an argless new Date() all throw inside a workflow. Workflows journal every agent() call so a run can resume, and non-determinism would invalidate that cache.' ray-amjad confirms the same banned list and that 'only what time is it now is blocked' (i.e. new Date(specificValue) works) and that the orchestrator has 'no filesystem and no Node.js APIs — no require, fs, process, network.' Official docs corroborate ONLY the coarse no-filesystem/no-shell-from-script rule, never the specific banned-identifier list or that they throw. Two independent community sources agree, raising confidence, but Anthropic has not confirmed; whether the guard is a pre-execution AST gate vs a runtime throw is undocumented.

verified_claims.claim: parallel(thunks) is a BARRIER (waits for all) and NEVER rejects: a thunk that throws resolves to null in the result array (hence the .filter(Boolean) idiom); it takes an array of thunks [() => agent(...)], not promises.
verified_claims.evidence: Community-only, but consistently attested by TWO independent sources. ray-amjad: parallel 'waits for all of them — it is a barrier... A thunk that throws resolves to null in the result array; the call itself never rejects, so .filter(Boolean) before use.' alexop.dev independently: 'It is a barrier' and 'A thunk that throws resolves to null rather than rejecting the whole call, so always .filter(Boolean) the results.' Official docs never name parallel() or document this failure semantic. Internally consistent and dual-sourced, but unverified by Anthropic.

verified_claims.claim: pipeline(items, ...stages) has NO barrier between stages (items stream independently); each stage callback receives (prevResult, originalItem, index); a stage that throws drops that item to null and skips its remaining stages.
verified_claims.evidence: Community-only, dual-sourced. ray-amjad: 'There is no barrier between stages... Every stage callback receives (prevResult, originalItem, index)... A stage that throws drops that item to null and skips its remaining stages.' alexop.dev independently: 'pipeline() streams items through stages independently, with no barrier between stages.' Official docs never name pipeline() or document the (prevResult, originalItem, index) signature. Internally consistent and corroborated across two sources, but not Anthropic-confirmed.

verified_claims.claim: Every script's first statement must be export const meta = {...}, a PURE LITERAL — no variables, calls, spreads, or template interpolation — and reserved keys __proto__/constructor/prototype are rejected; meta is read statically (before the body runs) to populate the approval dialog.
verified_claims.evidence: Community-only but dual-sourced for the pure-literal rule. ray-amjad: 'the object must be a pure literal: no variables, no function calls, no spreads, no template interpolation... Reserved keys (__proto__, constructor, prototype) are also rejected.' alexop.dev independently: 'Every script starts with a meta block that must be a pure literal.' The static-extraction rationale is reinforced by the OFFICIAL docs, which confirm the per-run prompt 'shows the planned phases' and offers 'View raw script' (Ctrl+G) — consistent with metadata being extractable without executing the body. But the meta literal syntax, the reserved-key rejection, and the AST-gating enforcement are NOT in official docs.

verified_claims.claim: No mid-run user input is possible — only agent permission prompts can pause a run.
verified_claims.evidence: Official docs limits table verbatim: constraint 'No mid-run user input', reason 'Only agent permission prompts can pause a run. For sign-off between stages, run each stage as its own workflow.' Fully official.

verified_claims.claim: Subagents a workflow spawns ALWAYS run in acceptEdits mode and inherit the user's tool allowlist regardless of the session's permission mode (file edits auto-approved); un-allowlisted shell/web/MCP tools can still prompt mid-run.
verified_claims.evidence: Official docs verbatim: 'The subagents the workflow spawns always run in acceptEdits mode and inherit your tool allowlist, regardless of your session's mode. File edits are auto-approved. Shell commands, web fetches, and MCP tools that aren't in your allowlist can still prompt you mid-run.' Fully official.

verified_claims.claim: workflow(nameOrRef, args?) runs another workflow inline with ONE level of nesting only — calling workflow() inside a child throws; the child shares the parent's concurrency cap, agent counter, and token budget.
verified_claims.evidence: Community-SINGLE-source. ray-amjad: 'Nesting is one level only — calling workflow() inside a child throws.' alexop.dev mentions workflow() exists ('runs another workflow inline') but does NOT corroborate the one-level-only limit or the shared-counter/cap/budget detail. Official docs never name workflow(). So the one-level-nesting rule rests on a single reverse-engineering source and is unverified by any second source or by Anthropic.

verified_claims.claim: budget is an injected object {total, spent(), remaining()} representing a shared token target; once spent reaches total, further agent() calls throw (WorkflowBudgetExceededError), and unguarded budget loops with no target sprint into the 1000-agent cap.
verified_claims.evidence: Community-SINGLE-source (ray-amjad) for the precise surface. Official docs mention only generic plan-usage/rate-limit accounting ('Runs count toward your plan's usage and rate limits'), never a budget object, spent()/remaining() methods, the WorkflowBudgetExceededError class, or a '+500k'-style directive. alexop.dev mentions budget 'exposes the token target' but not the spent()/remaining()/hard-ceiling/error-class detail. The shared-pool semantics and error class are plausible but unverified by any second source or by Anthropic.

verified_claims.claim: Additional runtime constants: script size cap 524288 bytes (512 KB, rejected before parsing), per-agent stall timeout 180000 ms (3 min, retried up to 5x), and a 30000 ms VM synchronous timeout.
verified_claims.evidence: All community-SINGLE-source (ray-amjad limits table): 'Script size | 524288 bytes (512 KB)', 'Per-agent stall | 180000 ms (3 min)', 'VM synchronous timeout | 30000 ms.' None appear in official docs, which publish ONLY the 16-concurrent and 1000-total caps. No second source corroborates these numbers. Notably the 'VM synchronous timeout' label is itself the strongest evidence the substrate is a vm-module-style in-process sandbox rather than the 'V8 isolate' the report's framing assumes — Anthropic uses neither term, saying only 'isolated environment.' Treat all three numbers as lower-confidence single-source.

verified_claims.claim: The orchestration runtime is a V8 isolate / LangGraph-style durable-execution substrate.
verified_claims.evidence: The 'V8 isolate' framing is NOT supported by any source and is partly contradicted. Official docs say only 'isolated environment' and (via Boris Cherny) 'JavaScript, running locally or in the cloud' — never 'V8 isolate.' The explicit 'V8 isolate vs microVM' language belongs to a DIFFERENT product (Cloudflare's 'Claude Managed Agents' agent-sandbox backend), not the workflow orchestration runtime — conflating them is a known error. The community 'VM synchronous timeout' evidence points to a vm-module in-process sandbox, not a separately provisioned V8 isolate. Separately, the LangGraph @entrypoint/@task/checkpointer substrate is THIS REPO'S design choice for the Python port, NOT a confirmed mirror of Anthropic's runtime (Anthropic has not disclosed the internal implementation). Any literal V8-isolate or LangGraph-substrate claim about Anthropic's runtime is speculative-to-contradicted.

verified_claims.claim: Workflows require Claude Code v2.1.154+, launched 2026-05-28 in research preview alongside Opus 4.8, on all paid plans plus API/Bedrock/Vertex/Foundry; disabled via /config toggle, disableWorkflows in settings.json, or CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_WORKFLOWS=1 (opt-OUT).
verified_claims.evidence: Official docs Note verbatim: 'Dynamic workflows are in research preview. They require Claude Code v2.1.154 or later and are available on all paid plans, with Anthropic API access, and on Amazon Bedrock, Google Cloud Vertex AI, and Microsoft Foundry.' Disable mechanisms confirmed verbatim: '/config' toggle, '"disableWorkflows": true', and 'CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_WORKFLOWS=1'. IMPLEMENTER NOTE: this is an opt-OUT env var on GA; the pre-GA community repo's CLAUDE_CODE_WORKFLOWS=1 opt-IN gate is now stale.

verification.overall_reliability_note: There is a clean, exploitable split between official-confirmed BEHAVIOR and community-reverse-engineered MECHANISM, and an implementer should treat them very differently. OFFICIAL-CONFIRMED (verified verbatim against code.claude.com/docs/en/workflows, the authoritative primary source): JavaScript script + runtime, control-flow inversion, the 16-concurrent and 1000-total caps, no-filesystem/shell-from-script, same-session-only resume with cached completed-agent results, no-mid-run-user-input, acceptEdits + allowlist inheritance, v2.1.154+ / research-preview / disable settings. Boris Cherny (HN bcherny, verified Anthropic) independently confirms 'JavaScript, running locally or in the cloud' on the Claude Agent SDK and notes 'more docs + technical details coming soon' — so deeper internals are officially undocumented.\n\nThe entire fine-grained API surface a future implementer would most want — exact primitive signatures (agent/parallel/pipeline/phase/log/budget/workflow), the content-hash-of-(prompt,opts) journal key, the determinism guard's banned-identifier list, the meta pure-literal/reserved-key rule, the 512KB/180s/30s constants, and the WorkflowAgentCapError/WorkflowBudgetExceededError class names — is COMMUNITY-REVERSE-ENGINEERED, not Anthropic-documented. Crucially, triangulation depth varies: parallel-never-rejects, pipeline-no-barrier, the meta pure-literal rule, and the determinism-bans are each attested by TWO independent sources (ray-amjad + alexop.dev) and are internally consistent (raise to partially-confirmed). But the content-hash cache-key formula, workflow() one-level-nesting, the budget object surface, and the numeric runtime constants rest on a SINGLE source (ray-amjad, probing a pre-GA binary) with no second corroboration (unconfirmed). The report's 'V8 isolate' and 'LangGraph substrate' framings are contradicted/speculative — V8-isolate language actually comes from an unrelated Cloudflare product, and the LangGraph mapping is this repo's own port-design choice, not a confirmed mirror of Anthropic's runtime. Skepticism flag: ray-amjad's probe predates GA and the enablement gate already drifted (opt-IN env var -> opt-OUT), so other single-source binary-probed constants may have shifted at GA and should be re-verified against a current build before being hard-coded.
